attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old man is admitted with a two-day history of right upper quadrant pain and fever. He has a medical history of myocardial infarction two months ago, for which two drug-eluting stents were placed. His medications include aspirin and clopidogrel.

On examination he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 110/80mmHg and temperature 38.2°C. Abdominal examination demonstrates There a tender mass in the right upper quadrant. The rest of the abdomen is soft. His ultrasound shows a distended, thick- walled gallbladder containing several stones. He rapidly improves with intravenous fluids and antibiotics.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Stop the clopidogrel for one week and proceed to cholecystectomy
Stop the aspirin for one week and proceed to cholecystectomy
Plan for elective surgery in greater than 4 months time
attributes.explanation:
Whilst the standard of care for patients admitted to hospital with acute biliary pain or inflammation is cholecystectomy during their admission, the enthusiasm for prompt surgery must be tempered in the light of any co-morbidities or medications.

It is important to understand that in this case the cholecystectomy, although indicated, is not a life saving intervention for this patient as he is currently stable and improving. The patient recently had an episode of myocardial infarction and is now on dual anti-platelet therapy (DAPT) with clopidogrel and aspirin. This risk of a further myocardial event if the patient underwent a surgical procedure within six months of the infarct would be considerable and is considered far more life threatening than acute biliary pathology. Furthermore, the fact that he has stents in place means he is at risk of acute stent thrombosis, which can be fatal. After the insertion of drug-eluting stent patients are required to be on DAPT for 6-12 months, with 12 months recommended if the risk of bleeding is low. Therefore, the earliest this man could appropriately stop one of the agents (clopidogrel most commonly) and go onto mono-therapy would be at least four months (but ideally ten months) after his admission. So stopping one of the agents for one week would not be appropriate. The cardiologists will be extremely reluctant to withhold his DAPT at any stage over the next 6-12 months and if surgery is planned, both the surgeon and the patient will need to be aware of the increased risks of haemorrhage remaining on the agents.

Discussions with the cardiologists would be essential if the patient’s condition deteriorated and some form of intervention was indicated. In such circumstances a percutaneous cholecystostomy might be a reasonable plan of management, with reduced risks of haemorrhage.

Therefore, the most appropriate answer would be to delay the surgery at least four months and consider an elective surgery after this time, based on his symptoms and risks from stopping one or both of the anti-platelets for surgery.
learningPoints: DAPT should be continued for 6-12 (ideally 12) months after drug-eluting cardiac stents are inserted.
Dual anti-platelet therapy usually consists of low dose aspirin and clopidogrel.
DAPT following cardiac stents should not be stopped for elective surgeries <6-12 months after stent insertion.
references: Antithrombotic therapy after endovascular and cardiac interventions. In: Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd. [online]. 2019. Available at: https://tgldcdp-tg-org-au.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/viewTopic?topicfile=antithrombotics-after-cardiac-interventions#toc_d1e82.

Howard CE, Nambi V, Jneid H, Khalid U. Extended Duration of Dual-Antiplatelet Therapy After Percutaneous Coronary Intervention: How Long Is Too Long? J Am Heart Assoc. 2019;8(20):e012639. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6818039/
